questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A 'mineral' in geology is best defined as a
options: naturally occurring, inorganic solid with a definite chemical composition and an ordered crystalline structure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Minerals are the building blocks of rocks.

**Reasoning.** A mineral is naturally occurring, inorganic, solid, homogeneous, with a definite (or narrowly varying) composition and an ordered internal (crystalline) atomic arrangement.

**Answer.** Naturally occurring inorganic crystalline solid.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A rock is
options: a naturally occurring solid aggregate of one or more minerals (or mineraloids)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rocks are mineral aggregates.

**Reasoning.** Most rocks (e.g. granite) are aggregates of several minerals; some (e.g. pure limestone) are essentially one mineral, but they are still aggregates formed by geological processes.

**Answer.** Aggregate of one or more minerals.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: On the basis of their mode of origin, rocks are classified into three main groups:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Genesis defines the three rock classes.

**Reasoning.** Igneous rocks crystallise from melt, sedimentary rocks form by deposition/lithification of sediments, and metamorphic rocks form by alteration of pre-existing rocks; the rock cycle links them.

**Answer.** Igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Igneous rocks are formed by the
options: solidification and crystallisation of molten magma or lava
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Igneous = 'from fire'.

**Reasoning.** Cooling of magma at depth gives coarse-grained plutonic rocks (e.g. granite); rapid cooling of lava at the surface gives fine-grained volcanic rocks (e.g. basalt).

**Answer.** Solidification of magma/lava.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Sedimentary rocks are formed mainly by
options: weathering, transport, deposition and lithification (compaction and cementation) of sediments
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sedimentary rocks record surface processes.

**Reasoning.** Pre-existing rocks are weathered, the debris is transported and deposited in layers, then compacted and cemented (lithified) into rock such as sandstone, shale and limestone; coal also forms in this environment.

**Answer.** Deposition and lithification of sediments.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Metamorphic rocks are produced when pre-existing rocks are altered by
options: heat, pressure and chemically active fluids while essentially remaining solid
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Metamorphism is solid-state change.

**Reasoning.** Elevated temperature and/or pressure (and fluids) recrystallise minerals and develop new textures without melting; e.g. limestone → marble, shale → slate, granite → gneiss.

**Answer.** Heat, pressure and fluids (solid state).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The most abundant group of rock-forming minerals in the earth's crust is the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Silicon and oxygen dominate the crust.

**Reasoning.** Silicates, built on the SiO₄ tetrahedron, make up the bulk of crustal rocks; feldspars alone form ~50%, followed by quartz, pyroxenes, amphiboles and micas.

**Answer.** Silicates.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Mohs scale of hardness is a relative scale running from
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mohs scale ranks scratch hardness.

**Reasoning.** It is an ordinal scale from talc (1, softest) to diamond (10, hardest); a higher mineral scratches a lower one. It is relative, not proportional.

**Answer.** 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: On Mohs scale, the hardness of quartz is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Quartz is a standard reference mineral.

**Reasoning.** Quartz sits at hardness 7 on Mohs scale; it scratches glass (~5.5) and feldspar (6) but is scratched by topaz (8).

**Answer.** 7.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Cleavage of a mineral is the
options: tendency to break along definite planes of structural weakness, producing smooth flat surfaces
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cleavage reflects internal bonding.

**Reasoning.** Where bonds are weakest, the mineral splits along parallel planes; e.g. mica shows perfect basal cleavage, galena cubic cleavage. It is diagnostic in identification.

**Answer.** Breaking along planes of weakness.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The smooth, curved 'conchoidal' fracture is characteristically shown by
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fracture is breakage not controlled by cleavage.

**Reasoning.** Quartz, lacking cleavage, breaks along curved shell-like (conchoidal) surfaces, as does volcanic glass; mica, galena and calcite instead show well-developed cleavage.

**Answer.** Quartz and glass.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The 'streak' of a mineral is the
options: colour of the mineral in bulk
colour of its fine powder, observed on a streak plate
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Powder colour is more constant than bulk colour.

**Reasoning.** Rubbing the mineral on unglazed porcelain leaves a streak whose colour is diagnostic (e.g. hematite gives a reddish-brown streak even when the lump is steel-grey).

**Answer.** Colour of the powder.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Lustre of a mineral describes
options: the way its surface reflects light (e.g. metallic, vitreous, pearly)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Lustre is an optical surface property.

**Reasoning.** It records the quantity and quality of reflected light; broad categories are metallic (e.g. galena, pyrite) and non-metallic (vitreous, resinous, pearly, silky, dull).

**Answer.** Appearance in reflected light.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A mineral specimen of mass 50 g displaces 20 cm³ of water. Its specific gravity is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $SG=\dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{mass of equal volume of water}}=\dfrac{m}{V\rho_w}$.

**Working.**
$$SG=\frac{50\ \text{g}}{20\ \text{cm}^3\times1\ \text{g/cm}^3}=2.5$$

**Answer.** 2.5.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The chemical composition of quartz is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Quartz is crystalline silica.

**Reasoning.** It is silicon dioxide, SiO₂, one of the commonest crustal minerals; hardness 7, no cleavage, vitreous lustre, conchoidal fracture.

**Answer.** SiO₂.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Feldspars, the most abundant mineral group in the crust, are
options: aluminosilicates of potassium, sodium and calcium
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Feldspars are framework aluminosilicates.

**Reasoning.** They form a solid-solution series — alkali feldspar (K, Na) and plagioclase (Na–Ca); hardness ~6, two cleavages near 90°. They dominate most igneous rocks.

**Answer.** K/Na/Ca aluminosilicates.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Calcite, the chief mineral of limestone, can be readily identified because it
options: effervesces (fizzes) with cold dilute hydrochloric acid and has a hardness of 3 with rhombohedral cleavage
shows conchoidal fracture and no cleavage
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Carbonates react with acid.

**Reasoning.** Calcite (CaCO₃) releases CO₂ with dilute HCl; it is hardness 3, shows perfect rhombohedral cleavage and strong double refraction.

**Answer.** Effervesces with dilute HCl, hardness 3.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: An 'ore mineral' is one from which
options: a metal (or valuable constituent) can be profitably extracted
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ore implies economic recovery.

**Reasoning.** A mineral becomes an ore mineral when the desired metal can be extracted economically, e.g. hematite for iron, chalcopyrite for copper, bauxite for aluminium.

**Answer.** A metal can be profitably extracted.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The valueless mineral matter associated with an ore, which has to be separated during processing, is called
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gangue is the unwanted fraction of the ore.

**Reasoning.** Minerals such as quartz and calcite accompanying the ore minerals are the gangue; mineral processing rejects them, the discarded material becoming tailings.

**Answer.** Gangue.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The two most important iron ore minerals are
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Iron oxides are the chief iron ores.

**Reasoning.** Hematite (Fe₂O₃, ~70% Fe, reddish streak) and magnetite (Fe₃O₄, magnetic, ~72% Fe) are the principal sources of iron; galena/sphalerite are Pb/Zn ores, bauxite an Al ore.

**Answer.** Hematite and magnetite.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Plutonic (intrusive) igneous rocks are generally coarse-grained whereas volcanic (extrusive) rocks are fine-grained because
options: magma cooling slowly at depth grows large crystals, while lava cooling rapidly at the surface gives small crystals
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Grain size records cooling rate.

**Reasoning.** Slow cooling at depth allows crystals time to grow (phaneritic, e.g. granite); rapid surface cooling yields fine (aphanitic, e.g. basalt) or glassy textures.

**Answer.** Slow vs fast cooling.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Granite is a
options: coarse-grained acidic (felsic) plutonic rock composed mainly of quartz, feldspar and mica
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Granite is the type felsic plutonic rock.

**Reasoning.** It crystallises slowly at depth, is rich in silica (quartz + alkali feldspar + mica), light-coloured and coarse-grained; widely used as dimension stone.

**Answer.** Coarse-grained acidic plutonic rock.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Basalt, which forms the Deccan Traps of India, is a
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Basalt is the commonest extrusive rock.

**Reasoning.** It is fine-grained, dark, mafic (rich in Fe, Mg, plagioclase, pyroxene) and erupts as fluid lava; vast flows built the Deccan Traps.

**Answer.** Fine-grained basic volcanic rock.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Igneous rocks are termed 'acidic' or 'basic' chiefly on the basis of their
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Silica percentage classifies igneous rocks chemically.

**Reasoning.** Acidic (felsic) rocks such as granite are silica-rich and light; basic (mafic) rocks such as basalt are silica-poorer and dark; ultrabasic rocks have <45% SiO₂.

**Answer.** Silica content.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A 'glassy' (vitreous) texture in an igneous rock such as obsidian indicates
options: very slow cooling deep in the crust
extremely rapid cooling (quenching) of lava, allowing no crystals to grow
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Texture mirrors crystallisation time.

**Reasoning.** When lava cools almost instantly (e.g. in water), atoms cannot organise into crystals and a natural glass forms; slower cooling gives aphanitic, then phaneritic textures.

**Answer.** Very rapid cooling (quenching).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Sandstone, siltstone and shale are examples of
options: clastic (detrital) sedimentary rocks classified mainly by grain size
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Clastic rocks are made of transported fragments.

**Reasoning.** They are classified by particle size — gravel→conglomerate, sand→sandstone, silt→siltstone, clay→shale; cementation binds the grains.

**Answer.** Clastic sedimentary rocks.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Limestone, rock salt and coal are examples of
options: chemically or organically formed (non-clastic) sedimentary rocks
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Non-clastic sediments form by chemical or biological processes.

**Reasoning.** Limestone precipitates/accumulates as carbonate, rock salt by evaporation of brine, and coal from accumulated plant matter — all without mechanical transport of clasts.

**Answer.** Chemical/organic sedimentary rocks.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The most characteristic primary structure of sedimentary rocks is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Layering reflects successive deposition.

**Reasoning.** Sediments accumulate in beds separated by bedding planes; other features include cross-bedding, ripple marks and graded bedding, all aiding interpretation of the depositional environment.

**Answer.** Bedding/stratification.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Foliation in metamorphic rocks such as slate, schist and gneiss is
options: the bedding inherited unchanged from sediments
a planar arrangement of mineral grains developed under directed (differential) pressure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Directed stress aligns minerals.

**Reasoning.** Platy/elongate minerals grow or rotate perpendicular to the maximum stress, giving slaty cleavage (slate), schistosity (schist) or gneissic banding (gneiss).

**Answer.** Pressure-aligned planar fabric.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Which pairing of parent rock → metamorphic product is correct?
options: Shale → slate and pure limestone → marble
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each protolith yields a typical metamorphic rock.

**Reasoning.** Shale → slate → phyllite → schist; pure limestone → marble; quartz sandstone → quartzite; granite → gneiss. Sandstone does not give marble, nor limestone quartzite.

**Answer.** Shale → slate; limestone → marble.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Coal is a combustible sedimentary rock formed by the
options: accumulation, burial and progressive alteration of plant (vegetal) matter
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coal is fossilised plant material.

**Reasoning.** Plant debris accumulates in swamps as peat; burial with increasing temperature and pressure drives coalification: peat → lignite → sub-bituminous → bituminous → anthracite.

**Answer.** Alteration of buried plant matter.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In the coalification (rank) series, the correct order of increasing rank is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rank rises with maturation.

**Reasoning.** As coalification proceeds, fixed carbon increases while moisture and volatile matter fall; peat (lowest) grades through lignite and bituminous to anthracite (highest rank).

**Answer.** Peat → lignite → bituminous → anthracite.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The 'rank' of a coal is a measure of its
options: degree of coalification (maturity), increasing with fixed carbon and decreasing with volatile matter and moisture
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rank expresses metamorphic maturity.

**Reasoning.** Higher-rank coals (anthracite) have more fixed carbon, higher calorific value and less volatile matter/moisture than low-rank coals (lignite). Rank is distinct from grade.

**Answer.** Degree of coalification.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The 'grade' of a coal, as distinct from its rank, refers to its
options: quality as judged by the amount of mineral matter / ash (and hence useful heat content)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Grade reflects purity, not maturity.

**Reasoning.** Grade is governed by ash (mineral matter) content; Indian non-coking coals are graded by gross calorific value. Two coals of the same rank can differ in grade.

**Answer.** Quality set by ash/mineral content.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The proximate analysis of coal determines
options: the elements C, H, N, S and O
moisture, volatile matter, ash and fixed carbon
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Proximate analysis groups coal into four fractions.

**Reasoning.** Standard heating/combustion steps give moisture, volatile matter and ash by difference/residue, with fixed carbon obtained as the remainder; it guides utilisation.

**Answer.** Moisture, VM, ash, fixed carbon.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The ultimate analysis of coal reports the
options: moisture, volatile matter, ash and fixed carbon
elemental percentages of carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, sulphur and oxygen
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ultimate analysis is elemental.

**Reasoning.** It gives C, H, N, S and O (O often by difference); these are needed to compute calorific value (Dulong) and combustion air/products.

**Answer.** C, H, N, S, O percentages.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A proximate analysis gives moisture 5%, volatile matter 25% and ash 10%. The fixed carbon, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FC=100-(\text{moisture}+VM+\text{ash})$.

**Working.**
$$FC=100-(5+25+10)=100-40=60\%$$

**Answer.** 60 %.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Macerals (vitrinite, liptinite/exinite and inertinite) are the
options: microscopic organic constituents of coal, analogous to minerals in inorganic rocks
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Macerals are coal's organic micro-components.

**Reasoning.** Derived from different plant tissues, vitrinite (woody), liptinite (spores/resins) and inertinite (oxidised) are identified under the microscope and control coking and other properties.

**Answer.** Microscopic organic constituents.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A coal seam covers an area of 1,000,000 m² with an average thickness of 3 m and an in-situ density of 1.4 t/m³. The in-situ tonnage, in million tonnes, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ area $\times$ thickness $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$T=1{,}000{,}000\times3\times1.4=4.2\times10^{6}\ \text{t}=4.2\ \text{Mt}$$

**Answer.** 4.2 million tonnes.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The bulk of India's coal reserves occur in rocks of the
options: Gondwana (Permian) Supergroup, e.g. the Damodar valley coalfields (Raniganj, Jharia)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Indian coal is mainly Gondwana in age.

**Reasoning.** Over 95% of reserves lie in Permian Gondwana sediments of the Damodar, Son, Mahanadi and Godavari valleys; younger Tertiary coals occur in Assam/north-east but are minor.

**Answer.** Gondwana (Permian) coalfields.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The 'true dip' of a bed is the
options: horizontal direction of the bedding plane
maximum angle of inclination of the bedding plane to the horizontal, measured perpendicular to the strike
depth of the bed below surface
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dip measures inclination.

**Reasoning.** True dip is the steepest angle the bed makes with the horizontal, in the direction at right angles to strike; any other direction gives a smaller apparent dip.

**Answer.** Maximum inclination ⟂ strike.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The 'strike' of a bed is the
options: direction of a horizontal line lying in the bedding plane
direction of maximum slope of the bed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Strike is a horizontal reference in the plane.

**Reasoning.** It is the compass bearing of the horizontal line on the inclined plane (the trace of the bed on a horizontal surface) and is perpendicular to the dip direction.

**Answer.** Horizontal line in the bedding plane.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: On a planar bed, the dip direction and the strike are always
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dip and strike are orthogonal.

**Reasoning.** The strike is horizontal in the plane; the true dip is measured in the vertical plane at right angles to it, so the two directions are perpendicular in plan.

**Answer.** Perpendicular.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bed has a true dip of 45°. In a vertical section whose direction makes 30° with the strike, the apparent dip, in degrees, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\delta_a)=\tan(\delta_t)\sin\theta$, where θ is the angle between the section and the strike.

**Working.**
$$\tan\delta_a=\tan45^{\circ}\times\sin30^{\circ}=1\times0.5=0.5$$
$$\delta_a=\tan^{-1}(0.5)=26.57^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** 26.57°.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Compared with the true dip, the apparent dip in any other vertical section is
options: always less than or equal to the true dip
questions.solution:
**Concept.** True dip is the maximum dip.

**Reasoning.** Since $\tan\delta_a=\tan\delta_t\sin\theta$ and $\sin\theta\le1$, the apparent dip cannot exceed the true dip; they are equal only when the section is perpendicular to strike (θ = 90°).

**Answer.** ≤ true dip.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A fold in rock strata is
options: a bend or flexure produced mainly by compressive forces
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Folds record ductile deformation.

**Reasoning.** Compressive (and sometimes shear) stresses buckle layered rocks into folds; the geometry (limbs, hinge, axial plane) describes their form.

**Answer.** A bend produced by compression.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In an anticline (as opposed to a syncline),
options: the youngest beds occur in the core and the fold closes downward
the beds arch upward and the oldest beds occur in the core
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Anticlines arch up; synclines sag down.

**Reasoning.** An anticline is convex-up with the oldest strata in its core; a syncline is concave-up with the youngest strata in its core. Anticlines are important hydrocarbon/structural traps.

**Answer.** Up-arched with oldest beds in core.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A fault is a
options: fracture surface along which appreciable relative displacement of the two sides has occurred
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Faults are fractures with slip.

**Reasoning.** Stress exceeding rock strength produces a break, and the blocks move relative to each other along the fault plane; the amount and sense of movement classify the fault.

**Answer.** Fracture with relative displacement.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In a normal fault,
options: the hanging wall moves up relative to the footwall under compression
the hanging wall moves down relative to the footwall, typically under tension (extension)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Normal faults extend the crust.

**Reasoning.** Under tension the hanging wall slips down the dip relative to the footwall, lengthening the section; grabens and horsts result from sets of normal faults.

**Answer.** Hanging wall down (extension).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A reverse (or thrust) fault is one in which
options: the hanging wall moves up relative to the footwall under compression
the hanging wall moves down under tension
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Reverse faults shorten the crust.

**Reasoning.** Compression drives the hanging wall up relative to the footwall; a low-angle reverse fault (<45° dip) is a thrust, common in fold-and-thrust belts.

**Answer.** Hanging wall up (compression).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A strike-slip (transcurrent) fault is characterised by displacement that is predominantly
options: horizontal, parallel to the strike of the fault
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Strike-slip motion is lateral.

**Reasoning.** The blocks slide past each other horizontally along the fault strike (e.g. transform faults); sense is described as dextral (right-lateral) or sinistral (left-lateral).

**Answer.** Horizontal, along strike.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A dip-slip fault has a net slip of 10 m along a fault plane dipping 30°. The throw (vertical component), in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Throw $=$ net slip $\times\sin(\text{dip})$.

**Working.**
$$\text{Throw}=10\sin30^{\circ}=10\times0.5=5.0\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 5.0 m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: For the same fault (net slip 10 m, dip 30°), the heave (horizontal component), in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Heave $=$ net slip $\times\cos(\text{dip})$.

**Working.**
$$\text{Heave}=10\cos30^{\circ}=10\times0.8660=8.66\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 8.66 m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Joints differ from faults in that joints are fractures
options: along which large displacement has occurred
along which there is little or no appreciable relative displacement
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Joints are fractures without slip.

**Reasoning.** They form by tension, cooling or unloading; though they show negligible movement, joint sets strongly control rock-mass strength, permeability, blasting and slope stability.

**Answer.** Fractures with negligible displacement.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: An unconformity in a rock sequence represents
options: a continuous deposition with no time gap
a buried erosion (or non-deposition) surface marking a significant gap in the geological record
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unconformities mark missing time.

**Reasoning.** Older rocks were uplifted/eroded (or deposition ceased) before younger rocks were laid on top; the surface between records a hiatus.

**Answer.** Buried erosion surface (time gap).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: An 'angular unconformity' is one in which
options: the beds above and below are parallel
tilted/folded older strata are overlain, across an erosion surface, by younger beds at a different angle
younger sediments rest on an igneous/metamorphic basement
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Angular unconformity shows discordant attitudes.

**Reasoning.** The lower beds were tilted/folded and eroded before the upper, gently dipping beds were deposited, so they meet at an angle; a disconformity has parallel beds, a nonconformity has sediments on crystalline basement.

**Answer.** Discordant (angular) contact.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Two vertical boreholes 100 m apart lie in the dip direction. The top of a seam is 50 m deeper in the down-dip borehole than in the up-dip one. The true dip of the seam, in degrees, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\text{dip})=\dfrac{\text{vertical difference}}{\text{horizontal distance (in dip direction)}}$.

**Working.**
$$\tan\delta=\frac{50}{100}=0.5\Rightarrow\delta=\tan^{-1}(0.5)=26.57^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** 26.57°.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The 'outcrop' of a bed is
options: the part of the bed exposed at (or just beneath) the ground surface
the deepest part of the bed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Outcrop is the surface trace of a bed.

**Reasoning.** It is where the bed meets the topographic surface; its plan pattern depends on the bed's dip and the relief, and is interpreted from geological maps ('rule of Vs').

**Answer.** Surface exposure of the bed.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: For a given true thickness, the width of outcrop of an inclined bed on flat ground is greatest when the bed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gentle dips spread the outcrop.

**Reasoning.** On level ground the outcrop width $=t/\sin\delta$ (t = true thickness); as the dip δ decreases, $\sin\delta$ falls and the width increases, becoming a minimum for a vertical bed.

**Answer.** Gentle dip.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The cross-section shows folded strata. Identify structures A (left, up-arched) and B (right, down-folded).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 10 45 Q 80 5 150 45 T 290 45' fill='none' stroke='#8a5a2b' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M 10 75 Q 80 35 150 75 T 290 75' fill='none' stroke='#b3793b' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M 10 105 Q 80 65 150 105 T 290 105' fill='none' stroke='#cf9a5a' stroke-width='3'/><text x='72' y='140' font-size='13'>A</text><text x='208' y='140' font-size='13'>B</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cross-section through folded beds

options: A is an anticline (up-arch) and B is a syncline (down-fold)
A is a syncline and B is an anticline
Both A and B are synclines
Both A and B are faults
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Up-arches are anticlines, down-folds are synclines.

**Reasoning.** Structure A is convex-up (crest) — an anticline; structure B is concave-up (trough) — a syncline. The beds curve continuously, indicating folding rather than faulting.

**Answer.** A anticline, B syncline.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: An 'ore deposit' is
options: any rock containing a trace of metal
a natural concentration of ore minerals large and rich enough to be mined at a profit
the waste rock of a mine
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Economic concentration defines an ore deposit.

**Reasoning.** Ore-forming processes enrich a metal far above its crustal average so that, given grade, tonnage and market, extraction is profitable; otherwise it is only a mineral occurrence.

**Answer.** Economically mineable concentration.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Chromite and nickel-copper sulphide deposits associated with basic/ultrabasic intrusions are examples of
options: magmatic (orthomagmatic) deposits formed by crystallisation and gravity segregation within a magma
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Some ores crystallise directly from magma.

**Reasoning.** Early-formed dense minerals (chromite) or immiscible sulphide liquids (Ni–Cu) settle and concentrate in mafic/ultramafic bodies, giving magmatic ore deposits.

**Answer.** Magmatic deposits.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Many vein-type metal deposits (e.g. Pb-Zn, Cu, Au veins) are formed by
options: deposition of minerals from hot, mineral-bearing aqueous (hydrothermal) solutions in fractures
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Hot fluids transport and precipitate metals.

**Reasoning.** Hydrothermal solutions, often of magmatic or circulating-meteoric origin, carry dissolved metals and deposit ore and gangue in fissures (fissure-filling veins) and replacement bodies as they cool/react.

**Answer.** Hydrothermal deposition.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Placer deposits of gold, cassiterite (tin) and diamond form by
options: mechanical concentration of heavy, chemically resistant minerals by flowing water or wind
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Density and durability drive placer formation.

**Reasoning.** Weathering releases heavy, hard, chemically stable minerals; running water (or wind) winnows the light material and concentrates the dense grains in stream beds, beaches and terraces.

**Answer.** Mechanical (placer) concentration.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Bauxite and laterite are typical
options: residual deposits formed by intense chemical weathering that removes soluble matter and leaves an insoluble enriched residue
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Weathering can concentrate ore in place.

**Reasoning.** In warm, humid climates leaching removes silica and alkalis, leaving Al/Fe-rich residues; bauxite (Al ore) and laterite are the classic residual deposits.

**Answer.** Residual (weathering) deposits.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Banded iron formations (BIF) and evaporite salt deposits are examples of
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Chemical sedimentation forms large ore bodies.

**Reasoning.** BIFs precipitated iron oxides/silica in Precambrian seas; evaporites (halite, gypsum, potash) precipitated as restricted basins dried up — both are sedimentary deposits.

**Answer.** Sedimentary ore deposits.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A 'syngenetic' ore deposit is one that
options: formed at the same time as the enclosing host rock
was introduced into pre-existing rock at a later time
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Timing relative to host rock classifies deposits.

**Reasoning.** Syngenetic deposits (e.g. BIF, magmatic segregations) form together with the host; epigenetic deposits (e.g. fissure veins) are emplaced later into existing rock.

**Answer.** Formed with the host rock.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A 'stratabound' ore deposit is one that is
options: confined to a particular stratigraphic unit or bed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stratabound ores honour stratigraphy.

**Reasoning.** The mineralisation is restricted to a specific bed/horizon (though it may be disseminated within it); strata-form deposits additionally lie parallel to bedding.

**Answer.** Restricted to a particular stratum.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The usual logical sequence of mineral exploration is
options: reconnaissance → prospecting → detailed exploration (including drilling) → evaluation
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Exploration narrows from regional to local.

**Reasoning.** Broad reconnaissance identifies favourable areas; prospecting locates targets; detailed exploration (mapping, geophysics, drilling) and sampling delineate and evaluate the deposit before mining.

**Answer.** Reconnaissance → prospecting → detailed exploration → evaluation.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Which of the following is a geophysical method of exploration?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Geophysics measures physical-property contrasts.

**Reasoning.** Seismic, gravity, magnetic, electrical/resistivity and electromagnetic surveys detect subsurface variations in elastic, density, magnetic, conductive properties; seismic refraction is one such method.

**Answer.** Seismic refraction survey.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A magnetic survey is particularly useful for exploring
options: magnetite-bearing iron ore bodies, which produce magnetic anomalies
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Magnetic surveys map susceptibility contrasts.

**Reasoning.** Strongly magnetic minerals (magnetite) distort the earth's field, giving anomalies that outline iron ore (and mafic/ultramafic bodies); weakly magnetic rocks give little response.

**Answer.** Magnetite iron ore.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Geochemical exploration locates buried mineralisation by
options: detecting anomalous concentrations of elements (including pathfinder elements) in soil, water, rock or vegetation
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dispersion haloes betray hidden ore.

**Reasoning.** Weathering disperses ore elements, forming geochemical anomalies; sampling soils, stream sediments, waters or plants and analysing for the ore or 'pathfinder' elements (e.g. As for Au) guides targeting.

**Answer.** Element anomalies in surface media.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Diamond core drilling is used in exploration chiefly to
options: recover a cylindrical core of rock for geological logging, sampling and assay
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cores give a continuous rock record.

**Reasoning.** A diamond bit cuts an intact cylinder that is logged for lithology, structure, alteration and ore, and sampled for assay; core recovery and RQD also indicate rock quality.

**Answer.** Recover core for logging/assay.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In a 100 cm core run, the sum of the lengths of intact core pieces each ≥ 10 cm long is 70 cm. The Rock Quality Designation (RQD), in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $RQD=\dfrac{\sum(\text{pieces}\ge10\ \text{cm})}{\text{total run}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$RQD=\frac{70}{100}\times100=70\%$$

**Answer.** 70 %.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The difference between a mineral 'reserve' and a mineral 'resource' is that a reserve is
options: the economically and legally mineable part of a resource under current conditions
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Reserves are the economic subset of resources.

**Reasoning.** A resource is a concentration with reasonable prospects of eventual economic extraction; a reserve is the portion that is demonstrably economic and mineable now (modifying factors applied).

**Answer.** Economically mineable part of the resource.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The United Nations Framework Classification (UNFC) classifies mineral reserves/resources using
options: three axes — economic viability, feasibility (project) status and geological knowledge
questions.solution:
**Concept.** UNFC is a three-dimensional scheme.

**Reasoning.** Each category is defined by codes on economic-social viability (E), field-project/feasibility status (F) and geological knowledge (G), giving a harmonised classification adopted in India.

**Answer.** Three axes (E, F, G).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In order of decreasing geological confidence, resource categories are usually termed
options: possible, probable, proved with possible highest
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Confidence falls as data density decreases.

**Reasoning.** Measured/proved has the closest spaced, most reliable data; indicated/probable is moderate; inferred/possible is the least certain, based on limited sampling.

**Answer.** Measured → indicated → inferred.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: An ore block measures 200 m × 100 m × 5 m and has a bulk density of 2.7 t/m³. The contained tonnage, in tonnes, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ volume $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$V=200\times100\times5=100{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$
$$T=100{,}000\times2.7=270{,}000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 270,000 t.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Which of the following are recognised ore-forming (mineral concentration) processes? (Select all that apply.)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ore deposits form by physical and chemical concentration.

**Reasoning.** Magmatic segregation, hydrothermal precipitation and mechanical (placer) sorting all concentrate valuable minerals. Schistosity is a metamorphic fabric, not an ore-forming process.

**Answer.** Magmatic, hydrothermal, placer.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Which of the following are geophysical methods used in mineral exploration? (Select all that apply.)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Geophysics maps subsurface physical-property contrasts.

**Reasoning.** Gravity, magnetic and seismic surveys (with electrical and electromagnetic methods) detect density, magnetic and elastic variations underground. Proximate analysis is a laboratory test of coal composition, not a geophysical method.

**Answer.** Gravity, magnetic, seismic.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: On the block diagram the red line marks the strike of the bed and the green arrow its dip direction. The dip direction is

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,60 150,40 190,80 70,100' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8'/><polygon points='30,60 70,100 70,160 30,120' fill='#93c5fd' stroke='#1d4ed8'/><polygon points='70,100 190,80 190,140 70,160' fill='#60a5fa' stroke='#1d4ed8'/><line x1='30' y1='60' x2='150' y2='40' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='118' y='34' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>strike line</text><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='128' y2='118' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#dpg)'/><defs><marker id='dpg' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#16a34a'/></marker></defs><text x='118' y='132' font-size='10' fill='#16a34a'>dip</text></svg>
figure.caption: Strike line and dip of a bed

options: perpendicular to the strike line, in the plane of the bed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dip is measured perpendicular to strike.

**Reasoning.** The strike is the horizontal line in the bedding plane; the true dip is the line of steepest descent, which lies in the plane at right angles to the strike.

**Answer.** Perpendicular to the strike line.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A fault plane (see figure) dips at $60^\circ$ and the horizontal displacement (heave) of a marker bed across it is 10 m. The throw (vertical displacement), in m, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='120' y1='20' x2='150' y2='170' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><g stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='4'><line x1='20' y1='70' x2='123' y2='70'/><line x1='147' y1='110' x2='220' y2='110'/></g><line x1='205' y1='70' x2='205' y2='110' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1'/><text x='208' y='95' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>throw</text><text x='150' y='160' font-size='9'>fault dip 60°</text></svg>
figure.caption: Normal fault offsetting a bed

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\dfrac{\text{throw}}{\text{heave}}=\tan(\text{fault dip})$.

**Working.**
$$\text{throw}=\text{heave}\times\tan60^\circ=10\times1.7321=17.32\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $17.32$ m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A planar bed drops 50 m vertically over a horizontal distance of 100 m measured perpendicular to strike (see figure). The true dip of the bed, in degrees, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='20' y2='130' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='80' y='148' font-size='10'>horizontal 100 m</text><text x='24' y='90' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>drop 50 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Vertical drop over horizontal run

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\text{dip})=\dfrac{\text{vertical drop}}{\text{horizontal distance}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{dip}=\arctan\!\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)=\arctan(0.5)=26.57^\circ$$

**Answer.** $26.57^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bed dipping at $30^\circ$ crops out across a horizontal surface with an outcrop width of 40 m measured perpendicular to strike (see figure). Its true (perpendicular) thickness, in m, is _____ (one decimal).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='100' x2='210' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='60' y1='100' x2='140' y2='100' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='5'/><text x='70' y='120' font-size='10'>outcrop width 40 m</text><line x1='60' y1='100' x2='102' y2='58' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='64' y='72' font-size='9' fill='#1d4ed8'>dip 30°</text></svg>
figure.caption: Outcrop width of a dipping bed

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a horizontal ground surface, true thickness $t=w\sin\delta$ (w = outcrop width measured perpendicular to strike, $\delta$ = dip).

**Working.**
$$t=40\sin30^\circ=40\times0.5=20.0\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $20.0$ m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In the section shown, tilted older strata are truncated by an erosion surface (dashed) and overlain by near-horizontal younger beds. This relationship is an example of an

figure.caption: Tilted beds truncated below sub-horizontal beds

questions.solution:
**Concept.** An angular unconformity shows a clear angular discordance across the break.

**Reasoning.** The older beds were tilted and eroded before the younger, gently dipping beds were deposited, so the bedding above and below the surface meet at an angle — the defining feature of an angular unconformity.

**Answer.** Angular unconformity.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bed has a true dip of $30^\circ$. A vertical section is cut in a direction making $30^\circ$ with the strike. Compute the apparent dip seen in that section (degrees).

figure.caption: Apparent dip in an oblique section
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,120 150,120 190,80 70,80' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='80' x2='150' y2='120' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='108' font-size='8'>true dip=30 deg</text><text x='28' y='150' font-size='8'>section at 30 deg to strike</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\text{apparent dip})=\tan(\text{true dip})\times\sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the section and the strike.

**Working.**
$$\tan\alpha=\tan30^\circ\times\sin30^\circ=0.5774\times0.5=0.2887\Rightarrow\alpha=16.1^\circ$$

**Answer.** $\approx 16.1^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A dip-slip fault has a net slip of $20\ \text{m}$ measured along a fault plane dipping at $30^\circ$. Compute the vertical throw (m).

figure.caption: Throw and heave of a fault
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='30' x2='170' y2='140' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5 3'/><g stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'><line x1='20' y1='70' x2='95' y2='95'/><line x1='120' y1='120' x2='210' y2='120'/></g><text x='28' y='152' font-size='8'>fault dip=30 deg, net slip=20 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Throw $=$ net slip $\times\sin(\text{fault dip})$.

**Working.**
$$\text{throw}=20\sin30^\circ=20\times0.5=10\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $10$ m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: For the same fault (net slip $20\ \text{m}$, fault dip $30^\circ$), compute the horizontal heave (m).

figure.caption: Horizontal heave of a fault
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='30' x2='170' y2='140' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5 3'/><g stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'><line x1='20' y1='70' x2='95' y2='95'/><line x1='120' y1='120' x2='210' y2='120'/></g><line x1='95' y1='110' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='28' y='152' font-size='8'>heave = horizontal offset</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Heave $=$ net slip $\times\cos(\text{fault dip})$.

**Working.**
$$\text{heave}=20\cos30^\circ=20\times0.8660=17.32\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 17.3\ \text{m}$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Along the dip direction, a marker bed drops $50\ \text{m}$ in elevation over a horizontal distance of $100\ \text{m}$. Compute the true dip (degrees).

figure.caption: Dip from a cross-section
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='60' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='60' x2='180' y2='60' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='180' y1='60' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='80' y='52' font-size='8'>100 m</text><text x='185' y='95' font-size='8'>50 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{dip}=\arctan\left(\dfrac{\text{vertical drop}}{\text{horizontal distance}}\right)$.

**Working.**
$$\text{dip}=\arctan\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)=\arctan(0.5)=26.57^\circ$$

**Answer.** $\approx 26.6^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Two vertical boreholes $40\ \text{m}$ apart (along the dip direction) intersect the same seam, with the seam $40\ \text{m}$ deeper in the down-dip hole. Compute the apparent dip along that line (degrees).

figure.caption: Dip from two boreholes
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='20' x2='50' y2='90' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='20' x2='150' y2='130' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='90' x2='150' y2='130' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'/><text x='28' y='150' font-size='8'>holes 40 m apart, seam 40 m deeper</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{dip}=\arctan\left(\dfrac{\Delta\text{depth}}{\text{spacing}}\right)$.

**Working.**
$$\text{dip}=\arctan\left(\frac{40}{40}\right)=\arctan(1)=45^\circ$$

**Answer.** $45^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A coal block covers a plan area of $100{,}000\ \mathrm{m^2}$ with seam thickness $2\ \text{m}$ and in-situ density $1.4\ \mathrm{t/m^3}$. Compute the in-situ (geological) reserve in tonnes.

figure.caption: Coal block volume to tonnage
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,110 150,110 190,80 80,80' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='40,110 40,125 150,125 150,110' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='150,110 150,125 190,95 190,80' fill='#9ca3af' stroke='#475569'/><text x='30' y='145' font-size='8'>area=1e5 m^2, t=2 m, density=1.4</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ area $\times$ thickness $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$T=100000\times2\times1.4=280000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** $280{,}000$ t.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: If the recovery (extraction) factor for the $280{,}000\ \text{t}$ in-situ block is $0.80$, compute the recoverable (mineable) reserve in tonnes.

figure.caption: Recoverable fraction of a reserve
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='40' width='160' height='50' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='30' y='40' width='128' height='50' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='40' y='70' font-size='9'>recover 80%</text><text x='30' y='115' font-size='8'>in-situ = 280000 t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Recoverable reserve $=$ in-situ reserve $\times$ recovery factor.

**Working.**
$$T_r=280000\times0.80=224000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** $224{,}000$ t.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In a core run of $100\ \text{cm}$, the sound core pieces each $\ge 10\ \text{cm}$ long total $75\ \text{cm}$. Compute the Rock Quality Designation (RQD) in percent.

figure.caption: RQD from a core run
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='45' width='200' height='20' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#b45309'><rect x='22' y='45' width='40' height='20'/><rect x='66' y='45' width='30' height='20'/><rect x='110' y='45' width='8' height='20'/><rect x='122' y='45' width='55' height='20'/></g><text x='28' y='90' font-size='8'>core run 100 cm; sound pieces shaded</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{RQD}=\dfrac{\sum(\text{intact pieces}\ge10\ \text{cm})}{\text{total run length}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$\text{RQD}=\frac{75}{100}\times100=75\%$$

**Answer.** $75\%$ (good rock mass).

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The map symbol shown (a long line with a short perpendicular tick and the number $30$) records the attitude of a bed. It means:
options: the long line is the strike, the tick points down-dip, and 30 is the amount of dip
the long line is the dip direction and 30 is the strike azimuth
the symbol marks a vertical borehole 30 m deep
the number 30 is the bed thickness

figure.caption: Strike and dip symbol
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='80' x2='140' y2='80' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='90' y2='105' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><text x='96' y='100' font-size='9'>30</text><text x='28' y='140' font-size='8'>strike and dip symbol</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A strike-and-dip symbol encodes both the strike line and the dip.

**Reasoning.** The long line is drawn along strike, the short tick points in the down-dip direction (perpendicular to strike), and the adjacent number gives the dip amount in degrees.

**Answer.** The long line is strike, the tick points down-dip, and 30 is the dip amount.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The fold in the figure has limbs that dip away from a central axis. This structure is:
options: an anticline, with the oldest beds in its core
a syncline, with the youngest beds in its core
a recumbent fold lying on its side

figure.caption: Fold with limbs dipping away from the axis
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='none' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'><path d='M20 120 Q110 50 200 120'/><path d='M20 135 Q110 70 200 135'/></g><line x1='110' y1='55' x2='110' y2='120' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='55' y='145' font-size='8'>limbs dip away from the axis</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** In an anticline the limbs dip outward (away) from the fold axis.

**Reasoning.** Such an up-arching fold brings the stratigraphically oldest beds to its core, the diagnostic feature distinguishing it from a syncline.

**Answer.** An anticline, with the oldest beds in its core.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In the section, sedimentary beds rest on an eroded surface developed across igneous/metamorphic (crystalline) basement. This relationship is:

figure.caption: Sediments over crystalline basement
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='90' width='180' height='50' fill='#9ca3af' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='20' y1='90' x2='200' y2='90' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5 3'/><g stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='3'><line x1='20' y1='70' x2='200' y2='70'/><line x1='20' y1='55' x2='200' y2='55'/></g><text x='55' y='120' font-size='9'>igneous / metamorphic</text><text x='28' y='40' font-size='8'>sediments over crystalline rock</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A nonconformity separates sedimentary strata from underlying crystalline (igneous or metamorphic) rock.

**Reasoning.** The basement was eroded before the overlying sediments were deposited, so younger bedded rocks lie directly on non-stratified crystalline rock.

**Answer.** A nonconformity.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Comparing the dip seen in an oblique vertical section (apparent dip) with the dip in a section perpendicular to strike (true dip), the apparent dip is always:
options: less than or equal to the true dip

figure.caption: True versus apparent dip
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,110 150,110 190,70 70,70' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='70' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='98' font-size='8'>true dip</text><text x='55' y='128' font-size='8'>apparent dip (oblique)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\text{apparent})=\tan(\text{true})\sin\theta$, and $\sin\theta\le1$.

**Reasoning.** Because $\sin\theta$ cannot exceed one, the apparent dip can never exceed the true dip; it equals the true dip only when the section is perpendicular to strike.

**Answer.** Less than or equal to the true dip.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bedding plane descends $50\ \text{m}$ vertically over a horizontal distance of $100\ \text{m}$ measured perpendicular to strike (figure). Compute the true dip (degrees, two decimals).

figure.caption: Dipping bed cross-section
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#a16207' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='20' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><text x='24' y='82' font-size='8'>drop 50</text><text x='98' y='135' font-size='8'>horizontal 100</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{dip}=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{vertical}}{\text{horizontal}}\right)$.

**Working.**
$$\delta=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)=\tan^{-1}(0.5)=26.57^\circ$$

**Answer.** $26.57^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The fold shown has limbs dipping away from the crest (figure). It is:
options: an anticline, with the oldest beds in the core and limbs dipping away from the axis
a syncline, with the youngest beds in the core
a monocline with a single flexure
a recumbent fold with a horizontal axial plane

figure.caption: Arched fold
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M20 110 Q110 30 200 110' fill='none' stroke='#a16207' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M20 125 Q110 50 200 125' fill='none' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='3'/><text x='72' y='132' font-size='8'>beds arch upward</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fold nomenclature.

**Reasoning.** Limbs dipping away from a crest, with the oldest strata exposed in the core, define an anticline.

**Answer.** An anticline, with the oldest beds in the core and limbs dipping away from the axis.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A marker bed is displaced across a fault so that the same horizon is $20\ \text{m}$ lower on the downthrown side (figure). Compute the vertical throw (m).

figure.caption: Normal fault offset
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='50' width='90' height='15' fill='#a16207'/><rect x='110' y='70' width='90' height='15' fill='#a16207'/><line x1='110' y1='30' x2='110' y2='130' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='24' y='130' font-size='8'>marker offset 20 m vertical</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Throw is the vertical component of fault displacement.

**Working.** The same horizon sits $20\ \text{m}$ lower across the fault, so the throw equals $20\ \text{m}$.

**Answer.** $20$ m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bed of true thickness $30\ \text{m}$ dips at $30^\circ$ and outcrops on flat ground (figure). Compute the outcrop width measured perpendicular to strike (m).

figure.caption: Outcrop of a dipping bed
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,120 160,120 120,40' fill='#a16207' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='160' y2='130' stroke='#2563eb'/><text x='58' y='145' font-size='8'>outcrop width?</text><text x='92' y='92' font-size='8'>t=30, dip 30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** On flat ground $w=\dfrac{t}{\sin\delta}$.

**Working.**
$$w=\frac{30}{\sin30^\circ}=\frac{30}{0.5}=60\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $60$ m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In the section shown, tilted older strata are truncated and overlain by flat-lying younger beds (figure). This surface is:

figure.caption: Angular unconformity
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g stroke='#475569'><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='200' y2='60'/><line x1='20' y1='72' x2='200' y2='72'/></g><path d='M20 90 Q110 70 200 90' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><g stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='90' y2='130'/><line x1='110' y1='100' x2='170' y2='125'/></g><text x='28' y='148' font-size='8'>tilted beds below, flat above</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unconformities mark gaps in the record.

**Reasoning.** Tilted, eroded older beds overlain by flat younger beds at an angular discordance define an angular unconformity.

**Answer.** An angular unconformity.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A vertical borehole passes through a bed dipping at $30^\circ$, giving a vertical intercept of $40\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the true (perpendicular) thickness of the bed (m, two decimals).

figure.caption: Vertical hole through a dipping bed
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='110' y1='20' x2='110' y2='140' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='40,60 200,100 200,115 40,75' fill='#a16207' stroke='#475569'/><text x='114' y='92' font-size='8'>vertical hole</text><text x='28' y='152' font-size='8'>intercept 40, dip 30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** True thickness $t=h\cos\delta$ for a vertical hole.

**Working.**
$$t=40\cos30^\circ=40\times0.8660=34.64\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $34.64$ m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: On the bedding plane shown, the horizontal line along the surface (figure) represents:
options: the strike, which is horizontal and perpendicular to the dip direction
the dip, the line of steepest descent
the plunge of a fold axis

figure.caption: Strike and dip on a plane
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='60' x2='160' y2='60' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='100' y1='60' x2='100' y2='100' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='100,100 95,90 105,90' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='46' y='50' font-size='8'>strike line</text><text x='106' y='90' font-size='8'>dip</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Strike and dip are orthogonal.

**Reasoning.** The horizontal line of intersection of the bedding plane with a horizontal surface is the strike, always perpendicular to the dip direction.

**Answer.** The strike, which is horizontal and perpendicular to the dip direction.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The fold shown has limbs dipping inward toward the trough (figure). It is:
options: a syncline, with the youngest beds in the core and limbs dipping toward the axis
an anticline, with the oldest beds in the core
a dome with beds dipping radially outward

figure.caption: Trough fold
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M20 40 Q110 120 200 40' fill='none' stroke='#a16207' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M20 25 Q110 100 200 25' fill='none' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='3'/><text x='76' y='130' font-size='8'>beds sag downward</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fold nomenclature.

**Reasoning.** Limbs dipping toward a central trough with the youngest beds preserved in the core define a syncline.

**Answer.** A syncline, with the youngest beds in the core and limbs dipping toward the axis.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Structure contours on a bed have a vertical interval of $10\ \text{m}$ and a horizontal spacing of $50\ \text{m}$ on the map (figure). Compute the dip (degrees, two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{dip}=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{vertical interval}}{\text{horizontal spacing}}\right)$.

**Working.**
$$\delta=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10}{50}\right)=\tan^{-1}(0.2)=11.31^\circ$$

**Answer.** $11.31^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The structure shown has a central block dropped down between two outward-dipping normal faults (figure). It is:
options: a graben, a block down-dropped between two normal faults
a horst, an up-thrown central block

figure.caption: Down-dropped block
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='50' width='50' height='15' fill='#a16207'/><rect x='90' y='90' width='40' height='15' fill='#a16207'/><rect x='150' y='50' width='50' height='15' fill='#a16207'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='4 3'><line x1='75' y1='40' x2='90' y2='120'/><line x1='145' y1='40' x2='130' y2='120'/></g><text x='38' y='140' font-size='8'>central block down-dropped</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Extensional fault blocks.

**Reasoning.** A central block lowered between two normal faults dipping toward each other is a graben; the opposite (up-thrown) block is a horst.

**Answer.** A graben, a block down-dropped between two normal faults.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bed outcrops with a map width of $100\ \text{m}$ (perpendicular to strike) on flat ground and dips at $30^\circ$ (figure). Compute the true thickness of the bed (m).

figure.caption: Outcrop width to thickness
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,110 140,110 100,50' fill='#a16207' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='122' x2='140' y2='122' stroke='#2563eb'/><text x='52' y='138' font-size='8'>map width 100, dip 30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** On flat ground $t=w\sin\delta$.

**Working.**
$$t=100\sin30^\circ=100\times0.5=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $50$ m.
